references.title: Analysis of First-Year University Student Dropout through Machine Learning Models: A Comparison between Universities

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Predicting Student Dropout During the First Year
extracted.Students:
3750 first-year engineering students from UAI

2201 first-year engineering students from Universidad de Talca
extracted.Data sources:
Student Demographics, 
Highschool History and Characteristics, Application data, University Admission Test Scores
extracted.Features: [Year, Gender, School, Admission, Nem, Ranking, Mat, Lang, Optional, Pps, Preference, Commune, Region, University]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
From description, data follows a standard ML pipeline: 

Binning and aggregations, Feature Selection etc..

Class Imabalances are addressed by Undersampling of the Majority Class.

Nominal Variables were One-Hot Encoded for Tree-based Models.

Both datasets are reduced to a small portion of their initial sizes due to the Data Cleaning Efforts.
extracted.Models:
kNN
SVM
Naive Bayes
DT
Random Forest
GBoost
MLP
Logistic Regression
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {F1-Score: 0,37 - GBoost, Recall: 0.58 - kNN, Precision: 0.28 - RF & GBoost, Accuracy: GBoost & RF}
extracted.Comments:
Optimal Combination of Hyper-Parameters for each algorithm were assessed via Gridd-Search Optimization.

Models are compared as by the results in Aggregate and, later, separated by University. Model performance seems to be better for UTalca than for UAI.

Authors also perform analysis of Feature Importance and realize that Mat is the most important variable
extracted.Limitations:
Homogenous set of students (Engineering despite the 2 Universities).

No comparison against university specific models.

Noo use of behavioral data (e.g. logs) which could be helpful in obtaining better predictions.

Uninspiring Model Performance.

annotations.text: The work uses multiple ML models on 2 Chilean universities with the goal of predicting the probability of first-year engineering students dropping out. Reulsts show that doing university-specific models would lead to better results, with Gradfient-Boosting approaches being among the top-performing.
In terms of interpretability, authors identify that students who achieve higher test scores are more likely to continue with their studies and not dropout.
